skill.name: podcast-generation
skill.description: Generate podcast-style audio narratives with Azure OpenAI Realtime API, WebSocket streaming, PCM to WAV conversion, and browser playback patterns.
skill.summary: Build Azure OpenAI podcast-style audio generation flows from backend streaming to frontend playback.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic combinations, but review found a Markdown-only skill that documents Azure OpenAI Realtime API usage. The API key, network endpoint, and base64 decoding examples are legitimate for the stated audio workflow, while shell execution and weak cryptography findings are false positives. Publish with warnings because the skill instructs users to handle external API credentials and send prompts to Azure OpenAI.

medium_findings.title: External Azure OpenAI API and credential handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for network and credential handling risk. The skill instructs users to configure an Azure OpenAI API key and connect to an Azure Realtime WebSocket endpoint. This is expected for the stated purpose, but users must store secrets safely and understand that prompt content is sent to an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly shows an API key variable and a WebSocket connection to Azure OpenAI. The behavior is legitimate for audio generation, but the security impact is real because secrets and user text are involved.

medium_findings.title: Missing explicit input and payload safety guidance
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW for operational hardening. The examples send a user prompt to the model and decode streaming audio chunks, but the skill does not document prompt size limits, audio size limits, timeout handling, or retention controls. This is not malicious, but implementers need safeguards for cost, denial of service, and data handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show unbounded prompt submission and audio decoding flow, but the file is documentation rather than executable code. The risk depends on how downstream users implement the pattern.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for the external command findings. The backticks at the cited locations are Markdown fences and inline code used to document environment variables, Python, JavaScript, and event names. No shell command execution is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows Markdown code formatting, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. The file contains no executable Ruby or shell script.

low_findings.title: Base64 decoding is audio processing, not obfuscation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for obfuscation findings. The Python example decodes Realtime API audio deltas, and the JavaScript example converts base64 WAV data into a browser Blob. This matches the documented audio transport format and does not hide code or payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text describes PCM audio chunks and WAV playback. No evidence found that the base64 values contain executable code or concealed instructions.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings not semantically confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithm findings. The cited lines describe the skill and reference architecture documentation. No hashing, encryption, signature, or cryptographic algorithm usage is present at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text does not contain cryptographic primitives or security-sensitive hashing logic. The scanner likely matched unrelated text.

low_findings.title: Critical heuristic combinations dismissed after context review
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for confirmed malicious intent. The combination of network access, credentials, and base64 handling is explainable by the advertised Azure OpenAI audio workflow. No evidence found of data exfiltration to an unrelated endpoint, evasion behavior, prompt injection text, or hidden execution instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The high-risk indicators are all visible in examples that implement the declared podcast generation feature. The reviewed file contains no conflicting intent or suspicious instruction text.

dangerous_patterns.title: API key use with external WebSocket service
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows an API key passed into an Azure OpenAI Realtime client and a WebSocket URL built from an endpoint. This is a necessary integration pattern, but it requires secret management, trusted endpoints, and user disclosure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API key and endpoint pattern is explicit in the documentation. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the endpoint is the expected Azure OpenAI service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Base64 audio decoding path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill decodes base64 audio chunks in backend and frontend examples. The reviewed context shows audio handling, but implementations should validate payload sizes and MIME types before playback.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The decoding operations are real and visible, but they are used for audio data rather than executable content. The remaining risk is implementation hardening.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable patterns for turning written content into podcast-style audio. This skill explains Azure OpenAI Realtime streaming, audio conversion, and playback from backend to frontend.
actual_capabilities: Shows required Azure OpenAI Realtime environment variables.
Explains WebSocket connection setup for GPT Realtime Mini.
Demonstrates collecting audio deltas and transcript deltas.
Describes converting PCM audio into WAV output.
Shows browser playback from base64 WAV data.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include runnable project files.
It depends on an Azure OpenAI Realtime deployment and API key.
It does not provide production authentication or rate limiting.
It references architecture and script files that are not included in this package.

use_cases.title: Prototype narrated articles
use_cases.description: Turn written articles into short spoken episodes for product demos or editorial experiments.

use_cases.title: Build audio features
use_cases.description: Add backend streaming and frontend playback patterns to an existing web application.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Realtime API workflows
use_cases.description: Compare session setup, audio events, transcript events, and WAV conversion before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a basic podcast flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the podcast-generation skill to outline a simple Azure OpenAI Realtime flow for turning text into WAV audio.

prompt_templates.title: Design the backend stream
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the podcast-generation skill to design a FastAPI backend that streams audio from Azure OpenAI and returns a playable WAV response.

prompt_templates.title: Connect frontend playback
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the podcast-generation skill to describe how a React frontend should request generated audio and play it safely in the browser.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a production workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the podcast-generation skill to review my podcast audio architecture for secret handling, endpoint validation, payload limits, and error handling.

output_examples.input: Create a design for generating a five-minute narrated product update.
output_examples.output: A backend-to-frontend workflow covering environment setup, Realtime session creation, audio event collection, PCM to WAV conversion, and browser playback.

output_examples.input: Review my current audio generation flow for missing pieces.
output: Confirm Azure endpoint and deployment configuration.
Validate generated audio size before playback.

best_practices: Keep Azure OpenAI API keys on the server side.
Set prompt length, audio duration, timeout, and response size limits.
Log operational errors without storing sensitive prompt text.
anti_patterns: Do not expose API keys in frontend code.
Do not send private user content to external services without consent.
Do not decode or play unbounded audio payloads.

faq.question: Does this skill generate audio by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance for using Azure OpenAI Realtime API to generate audio.

faq.question: Which model does the skill describe?
faq.answer: It describes GPT Realtime Mini through an Azure OpenAI Realtime deployment.

faq.question: Does it include runnable source files?
faq.answer: No. The package contains the skill document only, with examples inside the Markdown file.

faq.question: Can it help with frontend playback?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to convert base64 WAV data into a Blob and play it in the browser.

faq.question: What secrets are required?
faq.answer: The workflow requires an Azure OpenAI audio API key and an Azure OpenAI endpoint.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with warnings. The skill is documentation, but it involves external API calls and credential handling.
